cross_connections.display_label: Bullard book on Brazil — Charlotte's birthplace

narrative_blocks.header: Born Bahia Brazil while father was US Consul
narrative_blocks.body: Charlotte Henrietta Hill was born September 23, 1824 in Bahia Brazil while her father Henry Hill was serving as United States Consul to Bahia — a diplomatic post Thomas Jefferson had appointed her father to. Her Brazilian birth was rare among Connecticut-line Hooker descendants of the 1820s; the family returned to the United States during her childhood. She would marry Judge Oliver Phelps Jacobs in Buffalo New York November 22, 1843.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter's husband wrote book on Charlotte's birth country
narrative_blocks.body: Two generations after Charlotte's Brazilian birth, her granddaughter Annie Jacobs Bullard married Washington Irving Bullard (1881-1948) — who, among other accomplishments, published 'Brazil: The Promising Land,' a book about the very country where Charlotte had been born. Bullard also invented the modern loom used in textile mills worldwide and was a vocal promoter of women's civil rights. The Brazil-birth-to-Brazil-book full circle spanned exactly one century.

Charlotte and Judge Oliver Phelps Jacobs had three sons: Edward Hill Jacobs (1845-1922), Frederick Augustus Jacobs (1855-1934), and William Oliver Jacobs (1858-1893). The family settled in Killingly, Windham County Connecticut. Charlotte died March 25, 1896 in Killingly at age seventy-one and was buried at Westfield Cemetery, Danielson, Windham County.
